methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $16M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 12 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Foundation Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), post-money $3B+ (reported, raise in progress)

data.narrative:
Mimic Robotics is a Seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by an ETH Zurich spinout team, having raised $16M to date. The valuation universe includes 9 private peers (including 1X Technologies, Foundation Robotics, Divergent, Exotec, and Locus Robotics) and 1 public comparable, though narrow peer-stage filtering limits direct multiples analysis.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable triangulation approach. Using total raised ($16M) as a base proxy for current valuation and applying a typical Seed-to-next-stage multiplier of 2.5x yields an implied valuation of $40M. A peer-median approach is not feasible here: while 12 same-sector robotics companies exist in the comp universe, insufficient overlap in stage, valuation tier, and available multiples (fewer than 3 tight matches) makes any comparable multiple unreliable and potentially misleading. Consequently, peer data serves primarily as context rather than direct input.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $34M to $46M, with a median of $40M. This range reflects reasonable bounds around the stage-progression result, acknowledging both typical step-up expectations and early-stage margin of error. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow-set peer multiples, limiting cross-validation of the 2.5x multiplier, and the nascent nature of the robotics sector itself, where comparable deal flow and exit patterns remain sparse. Additional data on customer traction, IP differentiation, or recent financing signals would strengthen conviction in this range.
